From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Bowed string instrument

This article is about the musical instrument and its playing styles. For the Indian film, see [*Fiddle* (film)](/wiki/Fiddle_(film) "Fiddle (film)").

"Fiddler" redirects here. For other uses, see [Fiddler (disambiguation)](/wiki/Fiddler_(disambiguation) "Fiddler (disambiguation)").

A **fiddle** is a [bowed](/wiki/Bow_(music) "Bow (music)") [string](/wiki/String_instrument "String instrument") [musical instrument](/wiki/Musical_instrument "Musical instrument"), most often a [violin](/wiki/Violin "Violin") or a bass.[[1]](#cite_note-1) *Fiddle* is a colloquial term for the violin, used by players in all genres, including [classical music](/wiki/European_classical_music "European classical music"). Although in many cases *violin* and *fiddle* are essentially synonymous, the style of the music played may determine specific construction differences between fiddles and classical violins. For example, fiddles may optionally be set up with a [bridge](/wiki/Violin_construction_and_mechanics#Bridge "Violin construction and mechanics") with a flatter arch to reduce the range of bow-arm motion needed for techniques such as the double shuffle, a form of [bariolage](/wiki/Bariolage "Bariolage") involving rapid alternation between pairs of adjacent strings.[[2]](#cite_note-2) To produce a [brighter](/wiki/Timbre#Brightness "Timbre") tone than the deep tones of gut or synthetic core strings, fiddlers often use steel strings. The fiddle is part of many traditional ([folk](/wiki/Folk_music "Folk music")) styles, which are typically [aural traditions](/wiki/Music#Oral_and_aural_tradition "Music")—taught "[by ear](/wiki/Playing_by_ear "Playing by ear")" rather than via written music.[[3]](#cite_note-OKHistory-3)

**Fiddling** is the act of playing the fiddle, and **fiddlers** are musicians who play it. Among musical styles, fiddling tends to produce rhythms that focus on dancing, with associated quick note changes, whereas classical music tends to contain more [vibrato](/wiki/Vibrato "Vibrato") and sustained notes. Fiddling is also open to improvisation and embellishment with [ornamentation](/wiki/Ornament_(music)#Celtic_music "Ornament (music)") at the player's discretion, in contrast to orchestral performances, which adhere to the composer's notes to reproduce a work faithfully. It is less common for a classically trained violinist to play folk music, but today, many fiddlers (e.g., [Alasdair Fraser](/wiki/Alasdair_Fraser "Alasdair Fraser"), [Brittany Haas](/wiki/Brittany_Haas "Brittany Haas"), and [Alison Krauss](/wiki/Alison_Krauss "Alison Krauss")[[4]](#cite_note-4)) have classical training.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Fiddle&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

The medieval fiddle emerged in 10th-century Europe, deriving from the [Byzantine lira](/wiki/Byzantine_lira "Byzantine lira") ([Ancient Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greek_language "Ancient Greek language"): λύρα, [Latin](/wiki/Latin_language "Latin language"): *lira*, English: lyre), a bowed string instrument of the [Byzantine Empire](/wiki/Byzantine_Empire "Byzantine Empire") and ancestor of most European bowed instruments.[[5]](#cite_note-ebfiddle-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6)

Lira spread widely westward to Europe; in the 11th and 12th centuries European writers use the terms *fiddle* and *lira* interchangeably when referring to bowed instruments.[[5]](#cite_note-ebfiddle-5)

The violin in its present form emerged in early 16th-century [northern Italy](/wiki/Northern_Italy "Northern Italy"). The earliest pictures of violins, albeit with three strings, are seen in northern Italy around 1530, at around the same time as the words "violino" and "vyollon" are seen in Italian and French documents. One of the earliest explicit descriptions of the instrument, including its tuning, is from the *Epitome musical* by [Jambe de Fer](/wiki/Jambe_de_Fer "Jambe de Fer"), published in [Lyon](/wiki/Lyon "Lyon") in 1556.[[7]](#cite_note-7) By this time, the violin had already begun to spread throughout [Europe](/wiki/Europe "Europe"). The fiddle proved very popular among both street musicians and the nobility; the French king [Charles IX](/wiki/Charles_IX_of_France "Charles IX of France") ordered Andrea Amati to construct 24 violins for him in 1560.[[8]](#cite_note-8) One of these instruments, the *Charles IX*, is the oldest surviving violin.

Over the centuries, Europe continued to have two distinct types of fiddles: one, relatively square-shaped, held in the arms, became known as the [viola da braccio](/wiki/Viola_da_braccio_(instrument) "Viola da braccio (instrument)") (*arm viol*) family and evolved into the violin; the other, with sloping shoulders and held between the knees, was the [viola da gamba](/wiki/Viola_da_gamba "Viola da gamba") (*leg viol*) group. During the [Renaissance](/wiki/Renaissance "Renaissance") the gambas were important and elegant instruments; they eventually lost ground to the louder *viola da braccio* family.[[9]](#cite_note-groveviol-9)

## Etymology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Fiddle&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Etymology")]

The [etymology](/wiki/Etymology "Etymology") of *fiddle* is uncertain: it probably derives from the Latin *fidula*, which is the early word for *violin*, or it may be natively Germanic.[[10]](#cite_note-10)[*[verification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*]

The name appears to be related to Icelandic *[fiðla](/wiki/Fi%C3%B0la "Fiðla")* and also [Old English](/wiki/Old_English "Old English") *fiðele*.[[11]](#cite_note-11) A native Germanic ancestor of *fiddle* might even be the ancestor of the early Romance form of *violin*.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

In medieval times, *fiddle* also referred to a predecessor of today's violin. Like the violin, it tended to have four strings, but came in a variety of shapes and sizes. Another family of instruments that contributed to the development of the modern fiddle are the [viols](/wiki/Viol "Viol"), which are held between the legs and played vertically, and have fretted fingerboards.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

## Ensembles

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Fiddle&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Ensembles")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5f/Musiciens_pub_Gus_O%27Connor-Doolin.JPG/250px-Musiciens_pub_Gus_O%27Connor-Doolin.JPG)](/wiki/File:Musiciens_pub_Gus_O%27Connor-Doolin.JPG)

Fiddlers participating in a session at a pub in Ireland

In performance, a solo fiddler, or one or two with a group of other instrumentalists, is the norm, though twin fiddling is represented in some North American, Scandinavian, Scottish and Irish styles. Following the folk revivals of the second half of the 20th century, it became common for less formal situations to find large groups of fiddlers playing together—see for example the Calgary Fiddlers, [Swedish](/wiki/Music_of_Sweden "Music of Sweden") [Spelmanslag](/wiki/Spelmanslag "Spelmanslag") folk-musician clubs, and the worldwide phenomenon of [Irish sessions](/wiki/Irish_traditional_music_session "Irish traditional music session").[[14]](#cite_note-14)[[15]](#cite_note-15)

Orchestral violins, on the other hand, are commonly grouped in sections, or ["chairs"](/wiki/Orchestra#Organization "Orchestra"). These contrasting traditions may be vestiges of historical performance settings: large concert halls where violins were played required more instruments, before electronic amplification, than did more intimate dance halls and houses that fiddlers played in.

The difference was likely compounded by the different sounds expected of violin music and fiddle music. Historically, the majority of fiddle music was dance music,[[3]](#cite_note-OKHistory-3) while violin music had either grown out of dance music or was something else entirely. Violin music came to value a smoothness that fiddling, with its dance-driven clear beat, did not always follow. In situations that required greater volume, a fiddler (as long as they kept the beat) could push their instrument harder than could a violinist.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Various fiddle traditions have differing values.

### Scottish, with cello

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Fiddle&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Scottish, with cello")]

In the very late 20th century, a few artists successfully reconstructed the Scottish tradition of violin and "big fiddle", or cello. Notable recorded examples include Iain Fraser and Christine Hanson, Amelia Kaminski and Christine Hanson's Bonnie Lasses,[[16]](#cite_note-16) [Alasdair Fraser](/wiki/Alasdair_Fraser "Alasdair Fraser") and [Natalie Haas](/wiki/Natalie_Haas "Natalie Haas")' Fire and Grace,[[17]](#cite_note-17) and Tim Macdonald and Jeremy Ward's *The Wilds*.[[18]](#cite_note-18)

### Balkan, with *kontra*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Fiddle&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Balkan, with kontra")]

Hungarian, Slovenian, and Romanian fiddle players are often accompanied by a three-stringed variant of the [viola](/wiki/Viola "Viola")—known as the *[kontra](/wiki/Kontra "Kontra")*—and by [double bass](/wiki/Double_bass "Double bass"), with [cimbalom](/wiki/Cimbalom "Cimbalom") and [clarinet](/wiki/Clarinet "Clarinet") being less standard yet still common additions to a band. In Hungary, a three-stringed viola variant with a flat bridge, called the *[kontra](/wiki/Kontra "Kontra")* or *háromhúros brácsa* makes up part of a traditional rhythm section in Hungarian folk music. The flat bridge lets the musician play three-string chords. A three-stringed double bass variant is also used.

## Styles

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Fiddle&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Styles")]

To a greater extent than classical violin playing, fiddle playing is characterized by a huge variety of ethnic or [folk music](/wiki/Folk_music "Folk music") traditions, each of which has its own distinctive sound.

### Europe

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Fiddle&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Europe")]

#### Great Britain

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Fiddle&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Great Britain")]

* [English folk music](/wiki/Folk_music_of_England "Folk music of England") fiddling, including:
  + [Northumbrian](/wiki/Music_of_Northumbria "Music of Northumbria") fiddle style, which features "seconding", an improvised harmony part played by a second fiddler.
  + [Lakeland](/wiki/Lake_District "Lake District") or [Cumbrian](/wiki/Cumbria "Cumbria") fiddling has a repertoire largely based upon [hornpipes](/wiki/Hornpipe "Hornpipe") but also incorporates reels and jigs.[[19]](#cite_note-19)
  + [Yorkshire](/wiki/English_folk_music#Yorkshire "English folk music") fiddling is a combination of two main approaches: a clearly phrased and articulated performance with few slurs but sometimes with open string drones, similar to the playing of Cotswold Morris and Southern English country dance musicians; for more notey tunes, a more fluent style with irregular slurs which shift the accent around between on-beats and off-beats, similar to Northumbrian fiddle music.[[20]](#cite_note-20)
* [Scottish fiddling](/wiki/Scottish_fiddling "Scottish fiddling"), including:
  + [Shetland](/wiki/Shetland "Shetland") fiddling, which includes [trowie](/wiki/Trow_(folklore) "Trow (folklore)") tunes said to come from [peerie folk](/wiki/Little_people_(mythology) "Little people (mythology)"). The style is characterised by "ringing strings" and syncopated rhythms.
  + A North East (particularly [Aberdeenshire](/wiki/Aberdeenshire "Aberdeenshire") and [Moray](/wiki/Moray "Moray")) tradition strongly influenced by baroque violin technique with staccato and [Scotch snap](/wiki/Scotch_snap "Scotch snap") bowing techniques and [double stops](/wiki/Double_stops "Double stops").
  + A [Scottish Borders](/wiki/Scottish_Borders "Scottish Borders") tradition with a repertoire heavy in [hornpipes](/wiki/Hornpipe "Hornpipe") and with heavy use of [double stops](/wiki/Double_stops "Double stops").
  + A [Highland](/wiki/Scottish_Highlands "Scottish Highlands") tradition, highly influenced by the ornamentation and [mixolydian](/wiki/Mixolydian "Mixolydian") scale of the [Great Highland Bagpipe](/wiki/Great_Highland_Bagpipe "Great Highland Bagpipe"), as well as smoother bowing than other Scottish fiddle styles and a swinging of the 6/8 [jig](/wiki/Jig "Jig") rhythm.
  + A West Highland and [Hebridean](/wiki/Hebrides "Hebrides") Tradition, very closely related to the Highland tradition with major influence from the [Gaelic](/wiki/Scottish_Gaelic "Scottish Gaelic") song tradition.
  + An [Orkney](/wiki/Orkney "Orkney") tradition with simpler bowing and ornamentation but with tunes featuring [accidentals](/wiki/Accidental_(music) "Accidental (music)").[[21]](#cite_note-21)
* [Welsh fiddling](/wiki/Music_of_Wales "Music of Wales") (Welsh *Ffidil*; see [Ar Log](/wiki/Ar_Log "Ar Log")), a recently revived tradition.

#### Ireland

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Fiddle&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Ireland")]

* [Irish folk music](/wiki/Irish_traditional_music "Irish traditional music") fiddling including:
  + [Donegal](/wiki/Donegal_fiddle_tradition "Donegal fiddle tradition") fiddling from the northwest in [Ulster](/wiki/Ulster "Ulster"), which features [mazurkas](/wiki/Mazurka "Mazurka") and a Scottish-influenced repertoire including [Strathspey](/wiki/Strathspey_(dance) "Strathspey (dance)") and [Highland Fling](/wiki/Highland_Fling "Highland Fling") dances. Fiddlers tend to play fast and make heavy use of [staccato](/wiki/Staccato "Staccato") bowing and may from time to time "play the bass", meaning a second fiddler may play a melody an octave below where a first fiddler is playing it.
  + [Sligo](/wiki/Sligo "Sligo") fiddling from northern [Connacht](/wiki/Connacht "Connacht"), which like Donegal fiddling tends to be fast, but with a bouncier feel to the bowing.
  + [Galway](/wiki/Galway "Galway") fiddling southern [Connacht](/wiki/Connacht "Connacht"), which is slower than Sligo or Donegal traditions, with a heavier emphasis on ornamentation. Tunes are occasionally played in Eb or Bb to match the tonality of flat pipes.
  + [Clare](/wiki/County_Clare "County Clare") fiddling from northern [Munster](/wiki/Munster "Munster"), which tends to be played near the slower Galway tempo yet with a greater emphasis on the melody itself rather than ornamentation.
  + [Sliabh Luachra](/wiki/Sliabh_Luachra "Sliabh Luachra") fiddling from the southwest in [Munster](/wiki/Munster "Munster"), characterized by a unique repertoire of [polkas](/wiki/Polka "Polka") and [slides](/wiki/Slide_(tune_type) "Slide (tune type)"), the use of [double stops](/wiki/Double_stop "Double stop") and [drones](/wiki/Drone_(music) "Drone (music)"), as well as playing the melody in two octaves as in Donegal.[[22]](#cite_note-22)

### Americas

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Fiddle&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Americas")]

#### United States

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Fiddle&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: United States")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/74/Peter_Stampfel_08.jpg/250px-Peter_Stampfel_08.jpg)](/wiki/File:Peter_Stampfel_08.jpg)

[Peter Stampfel](/wiki/Peter_Stampfel "Peter Stampfel") from [The Holy Modal Rounders](/wiki/The_Holy_Modal_Rounders "The Holy Modal Rounders")

[American fiddling](/wiki/American_fiddling "American fiddling") is a broad category including traditional and modern styles:

##### Traditional

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Fiddle&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Traditional")]

* [Blues](/wiki/Blues "Blues") fiddling
* [Cajun](/wiki/Cajun_music "Cajun music") and [Zydeco](/wiki/Zydeco "Zydeco") fiddling
* [Native American](/wiki/Native_Americans_in_the_United_States "Native Americans in the United States") fiddling, including:
  + [Cherokee](/wiki/Cherokee "Cherokee")[[3]](#cite_note-OKHistory-3)
  + [Creek](/wiki/Muscogee "Muscogee")[[3]](#cite_note-OKHistory-3)
  + [Tohono O'odham](/wiki/Tohono_O%27odham "Tohono O'odham") [waila music](/wiki/Chicken_scratch "Chicken scratch"), a style heavily influenced by Mexican fiddling[[26]](#cite_note-26) and featuring irregular counts and harmonies in thirds, fourths, and sixths.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
* [Old time](/wiki/Old_time_fiddle "Old time fiddle") fiddling, including:
  + Fiddling from [Appalachia](/wiki/Appalachia "Appalachia"), the most well-known style today, featuring heavy use of droning and double-stops as well as syncopated bowing patterns.
  + [Athabaskan fiddling](/wiki/Athabaskan_fiddling "Athabaskan fiddling") of the Interior Alaska.
  + [Midwestern](/wiki/Midwest "Midwest") fiddling, highly influenced by [Scandinavian music](/wiki/Scandinavian_music "Scandinavian music").
  + [Ozarks](/wiki/Ozarks "Ozarks") fiddling, faster and crisper bowing than Appalachia.
  + [Texas](/wiki/Texas "Texas") fiddling, with influences from [Mexican](/wiki/Music_of_Mexico "Music of Mexico") fiddling and an emphasis on competitive playing.[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a2/Kenny_Baker-fiddle.jpg/250px-Kenny_Baker-fiddle.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kenny_Baker-fiddle.jpg)

    Bluegrass fiddler [Kenny Baker](/wiki/Kenny_Baker_(fiddler) "Kenny Baker (fiddler)")
  + [New England](/wiki/New_England "New England") fiddling, with strong influences from [Québécois](/wiki/Quebec "Quebec")/[French Canadian](/wiki/French_Canadian "French Canadian") and British repertoires.
  + [Northwest](/wiki/Pacific_Northwest "Pacific Northwest") fiddling, with influences from both Ozark and Midwestern fiddle styles, though with a strong emphasis on competitive playing like Texas fiddling.

##### Modern

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Fiddle&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Modern")]

* [Bluegrass](/wiki/Bluegrass_fiddle "Bluegrass fiddle") fiddling
* [Country](/wiki/Country_music "Country music") fiddling
* [Western swing](/wiki/Western_swing "Western swing") style fiddling[[27]](#cite_note-27)

#### Canada

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Fiddle&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Canada")]

Fiddling remains popular in [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada"), and the various homegrown styles of [Canadian fiddling](/wiki/Canadian_fiddling "Canadian fiddling") are seen as an important part of the country's cultural identity, as celebrated during the opening ceremony of the [Vancouver 2010 Winter Olympics](/wiki/Vancouver_2010_Winter_Olympics "Vancouver 2010 Winter Olympics").

* [Cape Breton fiddling](/wiki/Cape_Breton_fiddling "Cape Breton fiddling"), with a distinct Scottish influence
* [French Canadian](/wiki/French_Canadian "French Canadian") fiddling including "[crooked tunes](/wiki/Crooked_tune "Crooked tune")", that is, tunes with irregular beat patterns.
* [Métis fiddling](/wiki/M%C3%A9tis_fiddle "Métis fiddle"), of central and western Canada featuring strong French Canadian influence, but with even more "crooked" tunes.[[28]](#cite_note-28)
* [Newfoundland](/wiki/Newfoundland "Newfoundland") fiddling, also featuring many crooked tunes, colloquially termed ‘singles’ or ‘doubles’.
* [Maritimes](/wiki/Maritimes "Maritimes"), [Acadian](/wiki/Acadian "Acadian") or Downeast style of fiddling, which has many similarities to [Cape Breton fiddling](/wiki/Cape_Breton_fiddling "Cape Breton fiddling")
* [English Canadian](/wiki/English_Canadian "English Canadian") fiddling or Anglo-Canadian fiddling

[Silvestre Vargas](/wiki/Silvestre_Vargas "Silvestre Vargas") (1901–1985), fiddler of the [Mariachi Vargas](/wiki/Mariachi_Vargas "Mariachi Vargas") from 1921 to 1975, director from 1931 to 1955

[Mexican](/wiki/Music_of_Mexico "Music of Mexico") fiddling includes

* [Mariachi](/wiki/Mariachi "Mariachi")
* Son arribeño
* [Son calentano](/wiki/Son_calentano "Son calentano")
